title: Reading and Use of English – Part 1
description: Read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap.

content.text: As cities expand, the boundary between the natural world and the built environment is (0) becoming increasingly blurred. Many wild animals, far from being driven out, have (1) to thrive in urban settings. This process of adaptation involves significant changes in behavior and even physical traits. For instance, some bird species have (2) the pitch of their songs to be heard over the constant hum of city traffic. These 'urban survivors' have also learned to (3) use of human-made structures for nesting and shelter. However, life in the city is not without its (4) , as animals must navigate new dangers such as domestic pets and glass buildings. Ecologists are now (5) research to understand how these changes affect the long-term survival of various species. It appears that the ability to (6) to new food sources is key to their success. By observing these animals, we can gain valuable (7) into the resilience of nature and the ways in which life persists in the most unexpected places. As we continue to develop our urban landscapes, creating 'green corridors' will be (8) to ensuring that wildlife can move safely between fragmented habitats, maintaining the delicate ecological balance of our modern, rapidly growing metropolitan areas across the globe.

example.explanation: “Becoming blurred” is a standard collocation for a distinction that is disappearing.

questions.explanation: “Managed to [verb]” is used when something difficult is achieved.
“Altered” is the formal verb for making a change to something, like a song's pitch.
“Make use of” is a fixed idiomatic phrase meaning to utilize.
“Pitfalls” refers to hidden or unsuspected dangers or difficulties.
“Conducting research” is the most common formal academic collocation.
“Adapt to” is the specific biological term for changing to suit new conditions.
“Gain insights into” is a standard collocation for acquiring a deeper understanding.
“Instrumental to/in” is a formal way to say something is essential for a goal.
